examples: No “hide” or “isolate” function to temporarily remove nodes from view — forces manual re-filtering or full reset.
No indication whether copied names are mangled or demangled — critical for IDAPython/Python scripting where symbol resolution depends on naming conventions.

Add a small clipboard icon next to each symbol name that copies the *demangled* name + virtual address in IDAPython-compatible format (e.g., `idaapi.get_name(0x401000)` or `idaapi.get_func_name(0x401000)`).
❌ No quick-filter row beneath headers (like Excel or IDA Pro’s column filters) — slows down ad-hoc exploration of symbol properties.
❌ Filter presets or “saved views” not visibly exposed — limits repeatability for recurring analysis patterns (e.g., “all exec events with low confidence”).
“Source Patterns (0 available)” and “No source patterns extracted” indicate the tool hasn’t even parsed or decompiled anything yet — it’s just a graph and metadata shell.
Metadata summary at bottom (T0/T1/T2, namespaces, features) gives quick stats — helpful for prioritizing binaries or comparing complexity.
No timestamps or audit trail for when analyses were run — makes reproducibility and versioning difficult.
Integration of LLM-assisted analysis (“Ask about this binary…”) with confidence metrics (97% shown) — adds interpretive value for complex binaries.
Add per-node metadata overlays (e.g., small badges: “Analyzed 2025-04-05”, “Confidence: 89%”, “Partial”) visible on hover or in a side panel.
No indication of search scope — unclear if it searches node names, strings, addresses, or embedded content.
No distinction between internal vs. library calls — a major gap for RE work, especially when assessing attack surface or identifying external dependencies.
No risk scoring, sorting, or “hotspots” visualization — forces manual exploration even for high-priority areas.
Metadata summary at bottom (namespaces, features, edges) gives quick stats useful for triage — helpful for deciding which binary to dive into next.

Integrate a “Capture for Video” mode that auto-frames and exports high-res annotated screenshots with optional overlays (e.g., register values, call stack) — tailored for YouTube thumbnails or video annotations.

**Feature-rich metadata**: The bottom panel’s breakdown of namespaces, features, and connections (e.g., “6 state machine”, “82 rpc”) provides immediate analytical context without requiring manual digging.
No colorblind-friendly mode or alternative visual encoding (e.g., shapes, patterns, or labels) — risks excluding viewers with color vision deficiencies.
Some colors (e.g., magenta for state machine vs. red for cli command) are too similar in saturation and hue, which may cause confusion under low-contrast displays or when captured in video.
Slightly increase hue separation between state machine (magenta) and cli command (red) — perhaps shift state machine to a deeper purple or teal to avoid visual overlap.
No visible screenshot/export button or annotation tools — forces reliance on external tools like OBS or Snipping Tool, breaking workflow.
Exported visuals would lack context (no legend, no timestamp, no project metadata) — unprofessional for public writeups or CTF reports.
Auto-generate a “Quick Report” PDF or PNG on demand that includes graph + top features + namespace stats — replace manual screenshot stitching.
No visible one-click copy button for symbol names — forces manual selection and right-click copy, which is error-prone and slow during bulk scripting.
Implement a “Copy All Selected” context menu option that outputs a Python list of tuples: `[("symbol_name", 0x401000), ...]` — ideal for bulk scripting and automation.
No copy button, no selection range, no formatting preservation — essentially no clipboard interaction at all.
Implement a “Copy Selected Code” button and allow range selection with Ctrl/Cmd+C — essential for pasting into writeups, scripts, or CTF solutions.
Introduce a “Re-analyze Selected” context menu option and a global “Refresh Analysis” button with timestamp logging in the journal panel.
Search results not shown in graph or ranked — no visual feedback or context (e.g., highlighting nodes or edges matching “init”).
No visible multi-select, pinning, or grouping functionality — critical for power users managing complex state machines or cross-referencing attack surfaces.
Introduce shift/ctrl multi-select + right-click context menu with “Pin,” “Group,” and “Export Selection” — essential for forensic triage and report generation.
No visible call graph or call chain visualization when selecting a function — critical for tracing exploit paths or understanding control flow in complex binaries.

Add an interactive call graph panel that renders on node click, with recursive calls highlighted (e.g., via self-loop arrows or color coding) and edge weights based on call frequency or static analysis heuristics.

Introduce a “Trace Path” feature allowing users to select two functions and visualize the shortest or most probable execution path between them, with library calls visually differentiated (e.g., via icon or border style).
Add a “Security Hotspots” toggle that auto-filters and highlights functions related to crypto, auth, memory, and network syscalls — color-coded by risk (CWE/ATT&CK mapped).
No copy button, no syntax highlighting, no line numbers, no export options — completely lacks the functionality needed to extract code for blogs or YouTube.
Add a “Capture View” button (with dropdown: “Graph Only”, “Full Panel”, “Annotated”) + annotation layer (arrows, text, highlights) before export.
No visible schema/type node expansion — fields, offsets, or struct layouts are not displayed, making data structure reconstruction impossible.
No architecture context (32/64-bit, endianness, alignment) — critical for accurate struct definition and memory layout interpretation.
No ctypes/exportable definition generation — forces manual reconstruction, increasing error surface and slowing analysis.
Add a “Schema Explorer” pane that expands structs with field names, types, offsets, and total size — mirroring IDA Pro’s struct view or Ghidra’s data type manager.
Include architecture metadata (e.g., “x86_64, little-endian, 16-byte aligned”) and allow toggling between 32/64-bit layouts to validate struct padding and alignment.
No address or offset included in any visible copy context — forces cross-referencing with disassembler, breaking workflow continuity.
❌ No visible compound filter syntax (e.g., “type:cli_command AND cwe:XXX”) — forces manual iteration or external scripting.
Add a mini-filter bar under each column header (e.g., “TYPE: cli_command | event”) to enable rapid, multi-column filtering without leaving the table.
Introduce a “Save Filter Preset” button + dropdown to recall common RE workflows (e.g., “CWE-78 + type=cli_command”, “namespace=droid.* + attack=”) — essential for scaling analysis across binaries.
Introduce an “Offline Mode” toggle that forces the tool to use only pre-cached taxonomies, with a warning if a query requires uncached data — essential for field RE work.
Integration of Treesitter and ELF analysis hints at structured parsing — potentially useful for semantic understanding beyond raw disassembly.
Add a toggle for “Colorblind Mode” that overlays shapes (circle, square, triangle) or subtle textures on nodes to reinforce type distinction beyond color.
No visible loading indicators or progress states for taxonomy data — creates uncertainty during lookups (e.g., is it slow, broken, or just empty?).
“100% conf” values appear static and unexplained — raises suspicion of placeholder data or lack of dynamic confidence scoring.
On empty graph, auto-load the “daemon.*” namespace (since it’s highlighted in the sidebar) and show its top 5 connected features with vulnerability tags — don’t make the user guess where to start.
“Source Patterns” section is empty and explicitly says “No source patterns extracted” — implies no decompilation or source reconstruction, which is essential for educational content.

**Empty graph viewport is misleading**: The central canvas is blank despite 6/334 nodes being “in scene” — this creates confusion. Is the graph not rendering? Is it zoomed out? No visual feedback or loading indicator.

**Add a “Graph Loading” state + zoom indicator**: If the canvas is empty, show a subtle “Zoom in to see nodes” or “Loading graph…” message. If nodes exist but aren’t visible, auto-fit or highlight the bounding box.
Graph canvas is empty until user manually selects a namespace — no auto-load or “show me something interesting” default view for quick documentation.
No visible code viewer or disassembly pane — critical for RE work. You can’t copy code because there’s no code to copy.
Add a disassembly or pseudocode view (e.g., Ghidra-style or IDA-like pane) with line-number toggles, syntax highlighting, and copy/paste support.
No visible evidence of hex pattern or regex search — critical for RE workflows involving shellcode, opcodes, or string patterns.
Add regex/hex pattern toggle in search bar and highlight matching nodes/edges in the graph with color or glow.
No visible code viewer or disassembly pane — critical for RE work. Without actual code or assembly, you can’t copy snippets for writeups or videos.
Add a disassembly/decompilation view with syntax highlighting, line numbers, and a “Copy as Markdown” button that preserves formatting and optionally includes line numbers.
No visible collapse/expand controls for nodes or namespaces — critical for managing graph density during deep analysis.
Add a right-click context menu with “Collapse Namespace”, “Hide Node”, and “Reset View” options — essential for iterative exploration.
Layout appears static; no force-directed or hierarchical reorganization options to reduce clutter or emphasize relationships.

**Implement hover tooltips + query history**: Add tooltips for all metrics and feature labels. Below the “Ask about this binary…” input, show a collapsible history of recent queries with “Re-run” buttons — turning it into a true conversational assistant.
No undo/redo stack or history panel for graph manipulations — risky when experimenting with complex views.
Introduce a “Graph History” panel with undo/redo and snapshot functionality — enables safe experimentation without losing context.

**No conversation history or chat persistence**: The “Ask about this binary…” input feels isolated — no record of past queries, no ability to revisit or refine prior questions, breaking the “colleague” conversational flow.
No indication whether taxonomies are cached locally or fetched on-demand — critical for offline or air-gapped RE workflows.
No visible filter for security-relevant functions (crypto, auth, network, malloc/free) — critical for vulnerability triage.
Add a small spinner or “loading…” state next to taxonomy columns when data is being fetched, and show cache status (e.g., “CWE: cached locally” or “ATT&CK: fetching…”).
No visible analysis state per node (analyzed/partial/unknown) — critical for tracking progress in large binaries or during incremental reverse engineering.
Show a live results panel below search bar with ranked matches (e.g., “init found in 3 functions, 2 strings, 1 address”) and allow navigation via click.
No indication of recursion, call frequency, or edge weights — makes it impossible to prioritize hot paths or detect loops during vulnerability analysis.
Double-click behavior is ambiguous — tooltip says “expand neighbors,” but no visual feedback or animation confirms it happened; no way to verify if it’s recursive or shallow.

**Overloaded terminology without tooltips**: Terms like “T0: 279”, “conf: 97%”, “cli command”, “state machine” lack hover explanations — critical for beginners and even experts unfamiliar with this tool’s taxonomy.
Live-render toggle and viewport controls (zoom/pan) offer basic but essential interactivity for navigating large graphs.
Add explicit visual feedback on double-click (e.g., animated expansion, highlight of newly revealed nodes) and allow configurable behavior (zoom vs. expand).
No explicit “re-trigger analysis” button or context menu option — forces reliance on ingestion or global reload, which is inefficient for iterative RE.

weaknesses: No visible collapse/expand controls for nodes or namespaces — critical for managing graph density during deep analysis.
No undo/redo stack or history panel for graph manipulations — risky when experimenting with complex views.
No “hide” or “isolate” function to temporarily remove nodes from view — forces manual re-filtering or full reset.
suggestions: Add a right-click context menu with “Collapse Namespace”, “Hide Node”, and “Reset View” options — essential for iterative exploration.
Introduce a “Graph History” panel with undo/redo and snapshot functionality — enables safe experimentation without losing context.
reviews.fullText:
1. PASS  
2. Score: 7/10  

**Strengths**  
- Clean, dark-themed UI with intuitive legend and feature categorization (rpc, event, schema, etc.) aids rapid orientation.  
- Quick-action buttons (“Load Sample Graph”, “View Vulnerability Map”) provide immediate entry points for exploratory analysis — valuable for triaging unknown binaries.  
- Namespace-level stats and top features by connections offer high-level context without requiring deep graph traversal.  

**Weaknesses**  
- No visible collapse/expand controls for nodes or namespaces — critical for managing graph density during deep analysis.  
- No undo/redo stack or history panel for graph manipulations — risky when experimenting with complex views.  
- No “hide” or “isolate” function to temporarily remove nodes from view — forces manual re-filtering or full reset.  

**What I would change**  
- Add a right-click context menu with “Collapse Namespace”, “Hide Node”, and “Reset View” options — essential for iterative exploration.  
- Introduce a “Graph History” panel with undo/redo and snapshot functionality — enables safe experimentation without losing context.

weaknesses: No visible schema/type node expansion — fields, offsets, or struct layouts are not displayed, making data structure reconstruction impossible.
No architecture context (32/64-bit, endianness, alignment) — critical for accurate struct definition and memory layout interpretation.
No ctypes/exportable definition generation — forces manual reconstruction, increasing error surface and slowing analysis.
suggestions: Add a “Schema Explorer” pane that expands structs with field names, types, offsets, and total size — mirroring IDA Pro’s struct view or Ghidra’s data type manager.
Include architecture metadata (e.g., “x86_64, little-endian, 16-byte aligned”) and allow toggling between 32/64-bit layouts to validate struct padding and alignment.

2. Score: 7/10

3. Strengths  
- Clean, modern UI with intuitive navigation and contextual quick actions (e.g., “Load Sample Graph”, “View Vulnerability Map”) that lower the entry barrier for beginners.  
- Rich metadata display at bottom (namespaces, feature counts, confidence score) gives immediate context about binary complexity and extraction quality.  
- Suggested queries in the ANALYSIS panel are excellent for guiding novices toward high-value questions (e.g., “What is the attack surface of daemon.* namespace?”).

4. Weaknesses  
- No visible filter for security-relevant functions (crypto, auth, network, malloc/free) — critical for vulnerability triage.  
- “No nodes in scene” state is unhelpful without auto-loading a default security-focused view or suggesting which namespace to click first.  
- No risk scoring, sorting, or “hotspots” visualization — forces manual exploration even for high-priority areas.

5. What you would change  
- Add a “Security Hotspots” toggle that auto-filters and highlights functions related to crypto, auth, memory, and network syscalls — color-coded by risk (CWE/ATT&CK mapped).  
- On empty graph, auto-load the “daemon.*” namespace (since it’s highlighted in the sidebar) and show its top 5 connected features with vulnerability tags — don’t make the user guess where to start.

weaknesses: No visible code viewer or disassembly pane — critical for RE work. Without actual code or assembly, you can’t copy snippets for writeups or videos.
“Source Patterns” section is empty and explicitly says “No source patterns extracted” — implies no decompilation or source reconstruction, which is essential for educational content.
No copy button, no syntax highlighting, no line numbers, no export options — completely lacks the functionality needed to extract code for blogs or YouTube.
suggestions: Metadata summary at bottom (namespaces, features, edges) gives quick stats useful for triage — helpful for deciding which binary to dive into next.
Add a disassembly/decompilation view with syntax highlighting, line numbers, and a “Copy as Markdown” button that preserves formatting and optionally includes line numbers.

Integrate a “Capture for Video” mode that auto-frames and exports high-res annotated screenshots with optional overlays (e.g., register values, call stack) — tailored for YouTube thumbnails or video annotations.

2. Score: 4/10  

3. Strengths

- Suggested queries panel offers contextual CTF-style questions (e.g., “What does request_permission do?”), which could help guide new researchers or automate hypothesis generation.  
- Metadata summary at bottom (namespaces, features, edges) gives quick stats useful for triage — helpful for deciding which binary to dive into next.

5. What you would change

weaknesses: **Empty graph viewport is misleading**: The central canvas is blank despite 6/334 nodes being “in scene” — this creates confusion. Is the graph not rendering? Is it zoomed out? No visual feedback or loading indicator.

**Overloaded terminology without tooltips**: Terms like “T0: 279”, “conf: 97%”, “cli command”, “state machine” lack hover explanations — critical for beginners and even experts unfamiliar with this tool’s taxonomy.

**No conversation history or chat persistence**: The “Ask about this binary…” input feels isolated — no record of past queries, no ability to revisit or refine prior questions, breaking the “colleague” conversational flow.
suggestions: **Feature-rich metadata**: The bottom panel’s breakdown of namespaces, features, and connections (e.g., “6 state machine”, “82 rpc”) provides immediate analytical context without requiring manual digging.

**Add a “Graph Loading” state + zoom indicator**: If the canvas is empty, show a subtle “Zoom in to see nodes” or “Loading graph…” message. If nodes exist but aren’t visible, auto-fit or highlight the bounding box.

**Implement hover tooltips + query history**: Add tooltips for all metrics and feature labels. Below the “Ask about this binary…” input, show a collapsible history of recent queries with “Re-run” buttons — turning it into a true conversational assistant.
reviews.fullText:
1. PASS  
2. Score: 8/10  

3. Strengths  
- **Visual clarity & structure**: The interface is cleanly segmented — project sidebar, binary list, graph viewport, and analysis panel — making it easy to orient yourself during deep analysis.  
- **Contextual guidance**: Suggested queries (e.g., “How do daemon.* namespace and droid.* namespace interact?”) act as onboarding hooks for beginners while still being useful for experts exploring attack surfaces.  
- **Feature-rich metadata**: The bottom panel’s breakdown of namespaces, features, and connections (e.g., “6 state machine”, “82 rpc”) provides immediate analytical context without requiring manual digging.

4. Weaknesses  
- **Empty graph viewport is misleading**: The central canvas is blank despite 6/334 nodes being “in scene” — this creates confusion. Is the graph not rendering? Is it zoomed out? No visual feedback or loading indicator.  
- **Overloaded terminology without tooltips**: Terms like “T0: 279”, “conf: 97%”, “cli command”, “state machine” lack hover explanations — critical for beginners and even experts unfamiliar with this tool’s taxonomy.  
- **No conversation history or chat persistence**: The “Ask about this binary…” input feels isolated — no record of past queries, no ability to revisit or refine prior questions, breaking the “colleague” conversational flow.

5. What you would change  
- **Add a “Graph Loading” state + zoom indicator**: If the canvas is empty, show a subtle “Zoom in to see nodes” or “Loading graph…” message. If nodes exist but aren’t visible, auto-fit or highlight the bounding box.  
- **Implement hover tooltips + query history**: Add tooltips for all metrics and feature labels. Below the “Ask about this binary…” input, show a collapsible history of recent queries with “Re-run” buttons — turning it into a true conversational assistant.

weaknesses: No colorblind-friendly mode or alternative visual encoding (e.g., shapes, patterns, or labels) — risks excluding viewers with color vision deficiencies.
Some colors (e.g., magenta for state machine vs. red for cli command) are too similar in saturation and hue, which may cause confusion under low-contrast displays or when captured in video.
suggestions: Add a toggle for “Colorblind Mode” that overlays shapes (circle, square, triangle) or subtle textures on nodes to reinforce type distinction beyond color.
Slightly increase hue separation between state machine (magenta) and cli command (red) — perhaps shift state machine to a deeper purple or teal to avoid visual overlap.
reviews.fullText:
1. PASS  
2. Score: 8/10

3. Strengths:
- Color coding is semantically meaningful: rpc (green), event (orange), schema (blue), state machine (magenta), cli command (red), namespace (white) — each type has a distinct, consistent hue that aids quick visual parsing.
- Legend is present and clearly positioned above the graph area, making it easy to reference without cluttering the main view.
- Dark theme with vibrant accent colors is aesthetically pleasing for screenshots and video content — ideal for educational use (e.g., YouTube tutorials).

4. Weaknesses:
- No colorblind-friendly mode or alternative visual encoding (e.g., shapes, patterns, or labels) — risks excluding viewers with color vision deficiencies.
- Some colors (e.g., magenta for state machine vs. red for cli command) are too similar in saturation and hue, which may cause confusion under low-contrast displays or when captured in video.

5. What you would change:
- Add a toggle for “Colorblind Mode” that overlays shapes (circle, square, triangle) or subtle textures on nodes to reinforce type distinction beyond color.
- Slightly increase hue separation between state machine (magenta) and cli command (red) — perhaps shift state machine to a deeper purple or teal to avoid visual overlap.

weaknesses: No visible screenshot/export button or annotation tools — forces reliance on external tools like OBS or Snipping Tool, breaking workflow.
Graph canvas is empty until user manually selects a namespace — no auto-load or “show me something interesting” default view for quick documentation.
Exported visuals would lack context (no legend, no timestamp, no project metadata) — unprofessional for public writeups or CTF reports.
suggestions: Add a “Capture View” button (with dropdown: “Graph Only”, “Full Panel”, “Annotated”) + annotation layer (arrows, text, highlights) before export.
Auto-generate a “Quick Report” PDF or PNG on demand that includes graph + top features + namespace stats — replace manual screenshot stitching.
reviews.fullText:
1. PASS  
2. Score: 7/10

3. Strengths  
- Clean, dark-themed UI with intuitive color-coded node types (rpc, event, schema, etc.) — excellent for visual parsing during RE sessions.  
- “Load Sample Graph” and “Browse Source Patterns” provide quick onboarding and context — useful for educational or rapid triage workflows.  
- Integrated “Suggested Queries” panel offers guided exploration — great for beginners or when you’re stuck in a binary’s logic flow.

4. Weaknesses  
- No visible screenshot/export button or annotation tools — forces reliance on external tools like OBS or Snipping Tool, breaking workflow.  
- Graph canvas is empty until user manually selects a namespace — no auto-load or “show me something interesting” default view for quick documentation.  
- Exported visuals would lack context (no legend, no timestamp, no project metadata) — unprofessional for public writeups or CTF reports.

5. What you would change  
- Add a “Capture View” button (with dropdown: “Graph Only”, “Full Panel”, “Annotated”) + annotation layer (arrows, text, highlights) before export.  
- Auto-generate a “Quick Report” PDF or PNG on demand that includes graph + top features + namespace stats — replace manual screenshot stitching.
